***Abraham Lincoln: Vampire Hunter*** is a 2012 American [horror film](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film") directed by [Timur Bekmambetov](/wiki/Timur_Bekmambetov "Timur Bekmambetov"), produced by Bekmambetov, [Tim Burton](/wiki/Tim_Burton "Tim Burton"), and [Jim Lemley](/wiki/Jim_Lemley "Jim Lemley"), and written by [Seth Grahame-Smith](/wiki/Seth_Grahame-Smith "Seth Grahame-Smith"), who also serves as an executive producer alongside [Simon Kinberg](/wiki/Simon_Kinberg "Simon Kinberg"), Michele Wolkoff, and John J. Kelly. It is based on the [novel of the same name](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln,_Vampire_Hunter_(novel) "Abraham Lincoln, Vampire Hunter (novel)") by Grahame-Smith, depicting a fictionalized history of the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") with the eponymous [16th president of the United States](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") reimagined as having a secret identity as a lifelong [vampire hunter](/wiki/Vampire_hunter "Vampire hunter") fighting against a caste of [vampiric](/wiki/Vampire "Vampire") slave owners. [Benjamin Walker](/wiki/Benjamin_Walker_(actor) "Benjamin Walker (actor)") stars as [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") with supporting roles by [Dominic Cooper](/wiki/Dominic_Cooper "Dominic Cooper"), [Anthony Mackie](/wiki/Anthony_Mackie "Anthony Mackie"), [Mary Elizabeth Winstead](/wiki/Mary_Elizabeth_Winstead "Mary Elizabeth Winstead"), [Rufus Sewell](/wiki/Rufus_Sewell "Rufus Sewell"), and [Marton Csokas](/wiki/Marton_Csokas "Marton Csokas").

Filming began in [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") in March 2011, and [Henry Jackman](/wiki/Henry_Jackman "Henry Jackman") was hired to compose the film's score. Produced by [Bazelevs Company](/wiki/Bazelevs_Company "Bazelevs Company"), [Dune Entertainment](/wiki/Dune_Entertainment "Dune Entertainment"), and [Tim Burton Productions](/wiki/Tim_Burton_Productions "Tim Burton Productions"), the film was released on June 20, 2012, in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and then two days later in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), by [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox"). The film received mixed reviews; critics praised the visual style but criticized the overly serious and inconsistent tone. The film also [failed to meet expectations at the box office](/wiki/Box_office_bomb "Box office bomb"), grossing $116 million worldwide against a budget of $69-$99 million.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abraham_Lincoln:_Vampire_Hunter&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In 1818, [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") lives in [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") with parents [Nancy](/wiki/Nancy_Lincoln "Nancy Lincoln") and [Thomas](/wiki/Thomas_Lincoln "Thomas Lincoln"), who work at a [plantation](/wiki/Slave_plantation#Slavery "Slave plantation") owned by Jack Barts. There, Lincoln befriends a young [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American") boy, [William Johnson](/wiki/William_Henry_Johnson_(valet) "William Henry Johnson (valet)"), and intervenes when Johnson is beaten by a slaver. Because of this, the Lincolns are fired. That night, Lincoln sees Barts break into their home and attack Nancy, who falls ill and dies the next day.

Nine years later, Lincoln attempts to kill Barts at the docks in revenge, but discovers that he is a [vampire](/wiki/Vampire "Vampire"). Before Barts can kill him, Lincoln is rescued by Henry Sturges, whom he had met earlier at a bar. Sturges offers to teach Lincoln how to be a vampire hunter, and he accepts. After a decade of training, they travel to [Springfield, Illinois](/wiki/Springfield,_Illinois "Springfield, Illinois"). Sturges explains that the vampires in the United States descend from Adam, a powerful vampire who owns a plantation in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") with his sister, Vadoma. Sturges reveals the vampires' weakness is [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver"), and presents him with a silver pocket watch.

In Springfield, Lincoln begins hunting vampires named in letters by Sturges, befriends shopkeeper [Joshua Speed](/wiki/Joshua_Fry_Speed "Joshua Fry Speed") and meets [Mary Todd](/wiki/Mary_Todd_Lincoln "Mary Todd Lincoln"). Though Sturges warns him of forming close relationships lest his loved ones are attacked by vampires, Lincoln develops romantic feelings for Mary. Later, Lincoln successfully finds and kills Barts, but not before learning Sturges is also a vampire. Lincoln confronts Sturges, who reveals that Adam turned him and killed his wife, as his soul was impure, and hers was not. Vampires cannot kill each other, so Sturges needs hunters like Lincoln to get his revenge.

Feeling betrayed, Lincoln abandons his mission, but his actions have been noticed by Adam, who lures Lincoln to his plantation in the South by kidnapping Johnson. In the mansion, Adam hosts a ball that turns into a feeding frenzy where the vampires attack the slaves. Lincoln watches from outside and then breaks in to save the day, but he is captured by the vampires. Adam tries to recruit him to his cause to turn America into a nation of the undead, but Lincoln refuses before the two are rescued by Speed. They find safe passage to the North with the help of [Harriet Tubman](/wiki/Harriet_Tubman "Harriet Tubman").

Lincoln marries Mary Todd and begins his political career, campaigning to abolish slavery, despite Sturges warning him that the slave trade keeps vampires under control due to being their food source. After Lincoln's election as [President of the United States of America](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States_of_America "President of the United States of America"), he moves to the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") with Mary, where they have a son, [William Wallace Lincoln](/wiki/William_Wallace_Lincoln "William Wallace Lincoln"), who is later bitten by Vadoma as retaliation and dies.

The [civil war](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") erupts; after Confederate President [Jefferson Davis](/wiki/Jefferson_Davis "Jefferson Davis") convinces Adam to deploy his vampires to the front lines, Lincoln orders the confiscation of all available silver to be melted and cast into silver weapons. Speed seemingly betrays Lincoln by informing Adam about the train transporting these weapons, which Adam then attacks after setting fire to an upcoming [trestle](/wiki/Trestle_bridge "Trestle bridge"). During the fight, Speed is killed, and Adam learns that the train holds only rocks, revealing Speed's betrayal was a ruse to lure Adam into a trap. Lincoln uses his watch to punch Adam, killing him, before escaping with Sturges and Johnson as the burning trestle beneath the train collapses.

Meanwhile, Mary, Harriet Tubman, and many [freedom seekers](/wiki/Freedom_seekers "Freedom seekers") transport the silver to Gettysburg through the [Underground Railroad](/wiki/Underground_Railroad "Underground Railroad"). While delivering the silver to the army, Mary recognizes Vadoma and kills her by shooting a silver necklace bearing the sword of one of William's toy soldiers. Armed with silver weapons, the Union soldiers destroy the vampires and turn the tide.

Nearly two years later, on [April 14, 1865](/wiki/Assassination_of_Abraham_Lincoln "Assassination of Abraham Lincoln"), Lincoln celebrates the end of the war. Sturges informs him that the remaining vampires have fled the country and tries to convince Lincoln to become a vampire, who declines before leaving with Mary for a [play](/wiki/Our_American_Cousin "Our American Cousin"). In modern times, Sturges approaches a man at a bar in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), just as he once did with Abraham.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abraham_Lincoln:_Vampire_Hunter&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

The film *Abraham Lincoln: Vampire Hunter* was first announced in March 2010 when Tim Burton and Timur Bekmambetov paired to purchase film rights and to finance its development themselves. The book's author, [Seth Grahame-Smith](/wiki/Seth_Grahame-Smith "Seth Grahame-Smith"), was hired to write the script.[[11]](#cite_note-12) [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox") beat other studios in a bidding war for rights to the film the following October.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

In January 2011, with Bekmambetov attached as director, Benjamin Walker was cast as [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"). He beat [Adrien Brody](/wiki/Adrien_Brody "Adrien Brody"), [Josh Lucas](/wiki/Josh_Lucas "Josh Lucas"), [James D'Arcy](/wiki/James_D%27Arcy "James D'Arcy"), and [Oliver Jackson-Cohen](/wiki/Oliver_Jackson-Cohen "Oliver Jackson-Cohen") for the role.[[4]](#cite_note-ben-5) Additional actors were cast in the following February.[[6]](#cite_note-mary-7)[[8]](#cite_note-jimmi-9)[[13]](#cite_note-dominic-14) Filming began in March 2011 in [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana").[[4]](#cite_note-ben-5)[[13]](#cite_note-dominic-14) The film had a budget of $99.5 million and was produced in 3D.[[3]](#cite_note-fastlane.louisianaeconomicdevelopment.com-4)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abraham_Lincoln:_Vampire_Hunter&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Release")]

*Abraham Lincoln: Vampire Hunter* was originally scheduled to be released in 2D and 3D on October 28, 2011, but the release date was later pushed back to June 22, 2012.[[14]](#cite_note-open-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) The movie premiered in New York City on June 18.[[16]](#cite_note-17) *Abraham Lincoln: Vampire Hunter* also made an unconventional debut with a screening for troops deployed in the Middle East. The movie was screened to over 1,800 sailors aboard the Navy aircraft carrier [USS *Abraham Lincoln*](/wiki/USS_Abraham_Lincoln_(CVN-72) "USS Abraham Lincoln (CVN-72)"), just before its [Refueling and Complex Overhaul](/wiki/Refueling_and_Complex_Overhaul "Refueling and Complex Overhaul") (RCOH) at Newport News, Virginia.[[17]](#cite_note-18) Several of the film's stars attended the screening, including [Anthony Mackie](/wiki/Anthony_Mackie "Anthony Mackie"), [Erin Wasson](/wiki/Erin_Wasson "Erin Wasson") and [Benjamin Walker](/wiki/Benjamin_Walker_(actor) "Benjamin Walker (actor)"), who dressed in character as [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"). The screening marks the first time that a major motion picture made its debut for United States servicemembers.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abraham_Lincoln:_Vampire_Hunter&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abraham_Lincoln:_Vampire_Hunter&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Critical response")]

On [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") the film has an approval rating of 34%, based on 192 reviews with an average score of 4.9/10. The consensus reads that the film "has visual style to spare, but its overly serious tone doesn't jibe with its decidedly silly central premise, leaving filmgoers with an unfulfilling blend of clashing ingredients." [Emanuel Levy](/wiki/Emanuel_Levy "Emanuel Levy") of EmanuelLevy.com wrote that "Though original, this is a strenuous effort to combine the conventions of two genres."[[19]](#cite_note-tomatoes-20) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic") it has a "mixed or average" score of 42 out of 100, based on 35 reviews.[[20]](#cite_note-21) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "C+" on an A+ to F scale.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

[Richard Corliss](/wiki/Richard_Corliss "Richard Corliss") of *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine stated that "The historical epic and the monster movie run on parallel tracks, occasionally colliding but never forming a coherent whole."[[22]](#cite_note-23) [Christy Lemire](/wiki/Christy_Lemire "Christy Lemire") of [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") meanwhile, commented on the film's tenor and visual effects, saying "What ideally might have been playful and knowing is instead uptight and dreary, with a visual scheme that's so fake and cartoony, it depletes the film of any sense of danger," awarding the film a rating of 1.5 out of 4.[[23]](#cite_note-24) [Joe Morgenstern](/wiki/Joe_Morgenstern "Joe Morgenstern") of *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* agreed, writing, "Someone forgot to tell the filmmakers ... that the movie was supposed to be fun. Or at least smart."[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Joe Neumaler of *[New York Daily News](/wiki/New_York_Daily_News "New York Daily News")* gave the film a rating of 1 out of 5, writing, "This insipid mashup of history lesson and monster flick takes itself semi-seriously, which is truly deadly."[[25]](#cite_note-26) The title was praised by [Manohla Dargis](/wiki/Manohla_Dargis "Manohla Dargis") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, who added, "it's too bad someone had to spoil things by making a movie to go with it."[[26]](#cite_note-27)

Positive response, meanwhile, came from Marc Savlov of the *[Austin Chronicle](/wiki/Austin_Chronicle "Austin Chronicle")*, "*Abraham Lincoln: Vampire Hunter* has heart to spare, and the occasional silvered bayonet to run it through."[[27]](#cite_note-28) *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")* reviewer Scott Bowles remarked, "A stylish slasher of a movie, a monster flick that does its vampires right, if not their real-life counterparts," giving the film 2.5 out of 4.[[28]](#cite_note-29) Further acclaim came from [Joe Williams](/wiki/Joe_Williams_(film_critic) "Joe Williams (film critic)") of *[St. Louis Post-Dispatch](/wiki/St._Louis_Post-Dispatch "St. Louis Post-Dispatch")*, who called it "the best action movie of the summer", praised the film for presenting "a surprisingly respectful tone toward American values and their most heroic proponent", calling "the battlefield scenes [...] suitably epic" and commended leading star Benjamin Walker, "a towering actor who looks like a young Liam Neeson and never stoops to caricature."[[29]](#cite_note-30)

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abraham_Lincoln:_Vampire_Hunter&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Box office")]

*Abraham Lincoln: Vampire Hunter* grossed $37,519,139 at the domestic box office and $78,952,441 in international markets. It received a worldwide total of $116,471,580.[[2]](#cite_note-BoxOfficeMojo-3) On its opening domestic weekend, it grossed $16.3 million from 3,108 theaters. In its second domestic weekend, it suffered a 63.1% drop, while gaining one theater. On its third weekend, it lost nearly half of its theater count, and another 67.6% in gross.[[30]](#cite_note-31)
